text.title: Growth Experiment Design Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every growth experiment must enforce BEFORE it ships. These prevent "experiment without controls" — the single most common failure mode of growth orgs.

rule.statement: Hypothesis must follow the form: "If we [change X] for [segment Y], then [metric Z] will [increase / decrease] by [at least W%] within [observation window]." Reject vague forms ("we think this will help retention").

rationale.source: Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments (Kohavi, Tang, Xu)

rule.rationale: A measurable hypothesis forces a falsifiable claim. Vague hypotheses are post-hoc rationalized regardless of result.

rule.statement: Score every candidate experiment on Impact / Confidence / Ease (each 1-10) BEFORE engineering effort begins. Document the assumptions behind each score. Reject builds for experiments scoring < 5 average without explicit override note.
rule.rationale: ICE forces an explicit cost/benefit conversation. Teams that skip ICE ship the easiest experiments, not the highest-leverage ones.

rule.statement: Control group must be defined BEFORE rollout: traffic-split %, exclusion rules, holdout group if applicable. Define-after-rollout is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Controls defined post-rollout invariably skew toward "what makes the experiment look good." Pre-definition prevents segment cherry-picking.

rule.statement: Before launching, run a synthetic event playback that exercises every funnel step the experiment depends on. Verify counts, tags, and segmentation correctness. No experiment ships with un-playback'd instrumentation.
rule.rationale: Roughly 20-30% of growth experiments produce useless data because of instrumentation bugs caught only days after launch. Playback catches these in minutes.

rule.statement: Sample size is computed from MDE (minimum detectable effect), baseline conversion rate, and statistical power target (≥ 0.8). The experiment must commit to a run length that achieves required sample. Premature stopping is forbidden unless guardrails are tripped.
rule.rationale: Underpowered experiments report false negatives ~ 50% of the time at typical growth-team sample sizes. MDE-tied sizing makes underpowered runs visible upfront.

example.title: Sharp hypothesis with controls
example.code:
Hypothesis: If we add a "skip onboarding" link for users in segment "returning_within_30d", then "Day-1 active rate" will increase by at least 5% within 28 days.
Traffic split: 50/50 control vs treatment, holdout cohort = 5% for replay analysis. MDE: 5% absolute on baseline 42%. Required sample/arm: 4,210. Run length: 11 days. Instrumentation playback: PASS (events: skip_clicked, onboarding_completed, day1_active_pinged — all tagged correctly with variant_id). ICE: Impact 7 / Confidence 5 / Ease 8 = avg 6.7.
example.note: Illustrates r1-r5. Hypothesis is falsifiable, ICE done, control defined, instrumentation playback green, sample math tied to MDE.

example.title: Experiment without controls
example.code:
Tweet from PM: "We added a new onboarding flow last Tuesday. Retention is up 7% since then! Big win, shipping to 100%."
Problems: r1: no falsifiable hypothesis stated upfront. r3: no control group — 100% rollout means no comparison. r5: 7% measured at unknown sample / variance; could be noise, could be Tuesday baseline drift, could be season effect.
example.note: Illustrates the "experiment without controls" pattern this methodology blocks.
